skill.name: azure-ai-translation-ts
skill.description: Build TypeScript applications that use Azure AI Translator SDKs for text translation, language detection, transliteration, and document translation.
skill.summary: Use Azure AI Translator SDKs from TypeScript for text and document translation workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged command execution, weak cryptography, environment access, network use, and a critical combined heuristic. Review found a Markdown documentation skill with TypeScript and bash examples, not executable marketplace code; the command execution and weak-cryptography findings are false positives. The remaining low risk comes from examples that use Azure endpoints, environment-based credentials, SAS URLs, and a fixed local output file.

low_findings.title: Azure Credential Examples Require Secret Handling
low_findings.description: The skill shows use of environment variables for the Translator subscription key, region, and endpoint. This is expected for Azure SDK examples and no hardcoded secret was found, but generated applications must avoid logging or committing these values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly show expected credential configuration for Azure SDK clients. Risk is low because secrets are placeholders or environment variables, not embedded values.

low_findings.title: Network Calls Are Expected Azure Service Usage
low_findings.description: The skill references Azure Translator endpoints and SAS URLs for document translation. This network use matches the stated purpose, but users should review destinations and limit SAS token scope and lifetime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network destinations are Azure Translator or user-provided Azure Storage SAS URLs. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, with ordinary cloud-credential exposure risk.

low_findings.title: Fixed Local File Write Example
low_findings.description: The document translation example writes a translated result to a local file named translated.txt. The filename is fixed, so path traversal was not found, but generated code could overwrite an existing file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is explicit and uses a fixed path in a documentation example. The concern is limited to overwrite behavior if users reuse the snippet directly.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Misread as Commands
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across Markdown code blocks. These are fenced documentation examples and not executable skill code, so no command execution risk was confirmed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every reported location is inside Markdown fenced examples or fence delimiters. No runnable script or instruction executes those snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Heuristic
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts at the metadata description and stream conversion line do not show a hashing or encryption algorithm. No MD5, SHA-1, or related weak cryptographic operation was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe Azure document translation and Node stream handling. They do not contain cryptographic code or algorithm selection.

content.user_title: Build Azure Translation Apps in TypeScript
content.value_statement: Teams need reliable localization code without guessing Azure SDK routes and types. This skill gives focused TypeScript examples for Azure text and document translation.
actual_capabilities: Shows installation commands for Azure text and document translation SDK packages.
Demonstrates API key and DefaultAzureCredential authentication patterns.
Provides examples for text translation, language detection, transliteration, and supported languages.
Explains single document translation with multipart upload and streamed output.
Shows batch document translation with Azure Storage SAS URLs and status polling.
Lists relevant TypeScript types for text and document translation clients.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not a runnable translation service.
It does not provision Azure Translator or Azure Storage resources.
It does not include full production error handling or retry policy examples.
It does not validate current Azure pricing, quotas, or regional availability.

use_cases.title: Add Product Text Translation
use_cases.description: Use the text translation examples to add multilingual labels, messages, and support content to an application.

use_cases.title: Build Document Localization Pipelines
use_cases.description: Use the document translation examples to send files through Azure Translator and store translated outputs.

use_cases.title: Prototype Language Tools
use_cases.description: Use detection and transliteration examples to test language-aware workflows before building a full service.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Translator
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a TypeScript example that translates plain text from English to Spanish with Azure AI Translator.

prompt_templates.title: Add Language Detection
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add language detection before translation. Show how to handle a low confidence detection result.

prompt_templates.title: Translate Uploaded Documents
prompt_templates.scenario: Implement document translation in a backend service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a TypeScript endpoint that sends one uploaded text document to Azure document translation.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Batch Translation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with storage and status tracking.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a batch document translation workflow with short-lived SAS URLs, polling, and failure handling.

output_examples.input: Translate a greeting from English to Spanish and French.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a TypeScript Azure Translator client setup, a request with two target languages, and notes about error handling.

output_examples.input: Create a document translation flow for a support article.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes credential setup, document upload, target language selection, streamed output handling, and safe storage of the result.

output_examples.input: Check which languages are available for translation.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains how to call the supported languages endpoint and present language codes, names, and native names.

best_practices: Store Azure keys and endpoints in environment variables or a managed secret store.
Use short-lived SAS URLs with the minimum permissions needed for document translation.
Check unexpected responses before reading response bodies in generated SDK code.
anti_patterns: Do not commit Azure subscription keys, SAS URLs, or translated private documents.
Do not give batch translation containers broad write permissions for longer than needed.
Do not assume auto-detected language is correct for short or mixed-language text.

faq.question: Does this skill run translations by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides TypeScript guidance and examples for building applications that call Azure AI Translator.

faq.question: Which Azure packages does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers @azure-rest/ai-translation-text, @azure-rest/ai-translation-document, and related authentication patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with document translation?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes single document translation and batch document translation examples.

faq.question: Does it support Azure managed identity?
faq.answer: Yes. The document translation section shows DefaultAzureCredential as an authentication option.

faq.question: Are Azure credentials included?
faq.answer: No. The examples use placeholders and environment variables. Users must provide their own Azure configuration.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with low risk. The reviewed file is documentation, and the risky scanner findings were not confirmed as executable behavior.
